title: The Art of Writing Clearly
content:
Clear writing is clear thinking made visible. If you struggle to explain something in plain words, it's often a sign that you don't fully understand it yet.

The first rule of clear writing is to know your audience. Who are you writing for? What do they already know? What questions do they have? Shape every sentence with your reader in mind.

Use short sentences. They are easier to read. They are easier to understand. When in doubt, split a long sentence into two shorter ones.

Prefer concrete words over abstract ones. Instead of saying "a significant amount of time," say "three hours." Instead of "utilized," say "used." Specificity builds trust with your reader.

Edit ruthlessly. Your first draft is just the beginning. Cut every word that doesn't pull its weight. If a sentence survives the question "What would be lost without this?", it earns its place.

Read your work out loud. Your ear catches awkwardness that your eye misses. If you stumble while reading, your reader will too.
